concepts.title: Semi-Perimeter
concepts.conceptBody:
Semi-Perimeter of $\vartriangle \text{ABC}\left( \text{s} \right)$ 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{s}=\frac{\text{a}+\text{b}+\text{c}}{2} \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ s}=\text{a}+\text{b}+\text{c} \\ {} & 2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ s}-2\text{a}=\text{b}+\text{c}-\text{a} \\ {} & 2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ s}-2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}=\text{c}+\text{a}-\text{b} \\ {} & 2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ s}-2\text{c}=\text{a}+\text{b}-\text{c} \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Vector Product
concepts.conceptBody:
Vector product

Thus $\mathbf{a}\times \mathbf{b}=\operatorname{ab}\sin \theta \hat{\text{n}}$ where $\hat{\text{n}}$ is a unit vector perpendicular to the plane of $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ such that $\mathbf{a},\mathbf{b},\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ form a right-handed orientation.

When $\mathbf{a}$ or $\mathbf{b}$ or both are null vector or $\mathbf{a}$ is parallel to $\mathbf{b}$ then $\tilde{n}$ is not defined.

In this case, we agree to write $\mathbf{a}\times \mathbf{b}=\mathbf{0}$.

Geometrical interpretation of vector product

Let $\vec{a},\vec{b}$ be two non-zero, non-parallel vector represented by $\overrightarrow{\text{OA}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text{OB}}$ respectively and let $\theta $ be the angle between them. Complete the parallelogram OBCA. Draw $\text{BL}\bot \text{OA}$.

In $\Delta \text{OBL},\sin \theta =\frac{\text{BL}}{\text{OB}}=\text{BL}=\text{OB}\sin \theta =|\overrightarrow{\text{b}}|\sin \theta $ … (i)

Thus, $\vec{a}\times \vec{b}$ is a vector whose magnitude is equal to the area of the parallelogram having $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ as its adjacent sides and whose direction $\hat{n}$ is $\bot $ to the plane of $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ such that $\vec{a},\vec{b},\hat{n}$ form a right handed system. Hence $\vec{a}\times \vec{b}$ represents the vector area of the parallelogram having adjacent sides along $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$.

Thus, area of parallelogram $\text{OACB}=|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}}|$

Also, area of $\Delta \text{OAB}=\frac{1}{2}$ area of parallelogram $\text{OACB}$

$=\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}}|=\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{\text{OA}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{OB}}|.$

NOTE: By the term vector area of a plane figure we mean that a vector of magnitude equal to the area of the plane figure and direction normal to the plane of the figure in the sense of right handed rotation.

(ii) Properties: 

(a) Vector Product is not commutative: 

i.e. $\mathbf{a}\quad \mathbf{b}\ne \mathbf{b}\quad \mathbf{a}$ but 

$\mathbf{a}\quad \mathbf{b}=-\left( \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \mathbf{b} & \mathbf{a} \\ \end{array} \right)$

(b) $(\text{ma})\times \mathbf{b}=\text{m}(\mathbf{a}\times \text{b})=\text{a}\times (\text{m}\mathbf{b})$.

(c) if the vectors are collinear, then $\mathbf{a}\times \mathbf{b}=0$ $\because \theta =\mathbf{0}$ or $\pi $

As a consequence of above $\mathbf{a}\times \mathbf{a}=0$

(d) Vector product of orthonormal trial of unit vectors $\hat{i},\hat{j},\hat{k}$ 

Using the definition of the vector product, we obtain

$\hat{i}\times \hat{i}=\hat{j}\times \hat{j}=\hat{k}\times \hat{k}=\vec{0}$

$\hat{i}\times \hat{j}=\hat{k},\hat{j}\times \hat{k}=\hat{i},\hat{k}\times \hat{i}=\hat{j}$

$\hat{j}\times \hat{i}=-\hat{k},\hat{k}\times \hat{j}=-\hat{i},\hat{i}\times \hat{k}=-\hat{j}$

(e) $\mathbf{a\times b}$ in terms of unit vectors 

$\text{Let }\quad \text{a}={{\text{a}}_{2}}\mathbf{i}+{{\text{a}}_{2}}\mathbf{j}+{{\text{a}}_{3}}\mathbf{j}\quad \therefore \text{a}=\sqrt{\sum{\text{a}_{1}^{2}}}$

$\mathbf{b}={{\text{b}}_{1}}\mathbf{i}+{{\text{b}}_{2}}\mathbf{j}+{{\text{b}}_{3}}\mathbf{k},\quad \therefore \text{b}=\sqrt{\Sigma \text{b}_{1}^{2}}$

then $\mathbf{a}\times \mathbf{b}=\left( {{\text{a}}_{1}}\mathbf{i}+{{\text{a}}_{2}}\mathbf{j}+{{\text{a}}_{3}}\mathbf{k} \right)\times \left( {{\text{b}}_{1}}\mathbf{i}+{{\text{b}}_{2}}\mathbf{j}+{{\text{b}}_{3}}\mathbf{k} \right)$

or $ab~\sin~ \theta~ \hat{\mathbf{n}}={{a}_{1}}{{b}_{2}}\mathbf{i}\times \mathbf{j}+{{a}_{1}}{{a}_{3}}\mathbf{i}\times \mathbf{k}+{{a}_{2}}{{b}_{1}}\mathbf{j}\times \mathbf{i}+{{a}_{2}}{{b}_{3}}\mathbf{j}\times \mathbf{k}={{a}_{3}}{{b}_{1}}\mathbf{k}\times \mathbf{i}+{{a}_{3}}{{b}_{2}}\mathbf{k}\times \mathbf{j}$

$=\left( {{a}_{2}}{{b}_{3}}-{{a}_{3}}{{b}_{2}} \right)\mathbf{i}+\left( {{a}_{3}}{{b}_{1}}-{{a}_{1}}{{b}_{3}} \right)\mathbf{j}+\left( {{a}_{1}}{{b}_{2}}-{{a}_{2}}{{b}_{1}} \right)\mathbf{k}$ … (1)

Above can be expressed in determinant from as

$\mathbf{a}\times \mathbf{b}=\left| \begin{matrix} \mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\ {{\text{a}}_{1}} & {{\text{a}}_{2}} & {{\text{a}}_{3}} \\ ~{{\text{b}}_{1}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{2}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{3}} \\ \end{matrix} \right|$

(f) Sine of the angle between a and b 

From the value of a b derived above in (e) part and squaring it

${{a}^{2}}{{b}^{2}}{{\sin }^{2}}\theta =\Sigma {{\left( {{a}_{2}}{{b}_{2}}-{{a}_{3}}{{b}_{2}} \right)}^{2}}$

$\therefore \quad {{\sin }^{2}}\theta =\frac{\Sigma {{\left( {{a}_{2}}{{b}_{3}}-{{a}_{3}}{{b}_{2}} \right)}^{2}}}{\Sigma a_{1}^{2}\cdot \Sigma b_{1}^{2}}$

(g) Condition for vectors to be parallel. 

In this case $\mathbf{a}\times \mathbf{b}=\mathbf{0}$ as $\theta =0$ or $\pi $ and hence from (e) the last two rows of the determinant must have their corresponding elements proportional.

(h) Distributivity of vector product over vector addition) 

Let $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ be any three vectors. Then

(i) $\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times (\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}})=\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}}+\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}}$ (Left distributivity)

(ii) $(\vec{b}+\vec{c})\times \vec{a}=\vec{b}\times \vec{a}+\vec{c}\times \vec{a}$ (Right distributivity)

(i) For any three vectors $\mathbf{\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}}$, we have 

$\vec{a}\times (\vec{b}-\vec{c})=\vec{a}\times \vec{b}-\vec{a}\times \vec{c}$

Remark: It follows from the above property that $\vec{a}\times \vec{a}=\vec{0}$ for every non-zero vector $\vec{a}$ which in turn implies that $\hat{\text{i}}\times \hat{\text{i}}=\hat{\text{j}}\times \hat{\text{j}}=\hat{\text{k}}\times \hat{\text{k}}=\vec{0}$.

VECTORS NORMAL TO THE PLANE OF TWO GIVEN VECTORS

Let $\vec{a},\vec{b}$ be two non-zero, non-parallel vectors and let $\theta $ be the angle between them.

$\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}}=|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}||\overrightarrow{\text{b}}|\sin \theta \hat{\text{n}}$

where $\hat{n}$ is a unit vector $\bot $ to the plane of $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ such that $\vec{a},\vec{b},\hat{n}$ form a right-handed system

$\Rightarrow \quad (\vec{a}\times \vec{b})=|\vec{a}\times \vec{b}|\hat{n}\Rightarrow \hat{n}=\frac{\vec{a}\times \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}\times \vec{b}|}$

Thus, $\frac{\vec{a}\times \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}\times \vec{b}|}$ is a unit vector $\bot$ to the plane of $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$.

Note that $-\frac{\vec{a}\times \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}\times \vec{b}|}$ is also a unit vector $\bot$ to the plane of $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$.

$\text{A vector of magnitud{e}'}\lambda '\text{ normal to the plane of } \vec{a} \text{ and } \vec{b} \text{ is given by }\pm \frac{\lambda (\vec{a}\times \vec{b})}{|\vec{a}\times \vec{b}|}$

SOME IMPORTANT RESULTS

1. The area of a parallelogram with adjacent sides $\vec{a}$ and $(\vec{b})$ is $|\vec{a}\times \vec{b}|$

2. The area of a triangle with adjacent sides $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ is $\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a}\times \vec{b}|$.

3. The area of a triangle $\text{ABC}$ is $\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{\text{AB}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{BA}}|$ or $\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{\text{CB}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{CA}}|$.

4. The area of a parallelogram with diagonals $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ is $\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a}\times \vec{b}|$.

5. The area of a plane quadrilateral $\text{ABCD}$ is $\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{\text{AC}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{BD}}|$, where $\text{AC}$ and $\text{BD}$ are its diagonals.

LAGRANGE’S IDENTITY

If $\text{AC}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text{a}},\overrightarrow{\text{b}}$ be any two vectors, then

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \quad |\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}}=|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}{{|}^{2}}|\overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}}-{{(\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{b}})}^{2}} \\ \text{ or }\quad |\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}}+{{(\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{b}})}^{2}}=|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}{{|}^{2}}|\overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}} \\ \end{array}$

Proof. We have

$|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}}|=|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}||\overrightarrow{\text{b}}|\sin \theta $

$\therefore $ $|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}}=|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}{{|}^{2}}|\overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}}{{\sin }^{2}}\theta $

$=|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}{{|}^{2}}|\overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}}\left( 1-{{\cos }^{2}}\theta \right)$

$=|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}{{|}^{2}}|\overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}}-|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}{{|}^{2}}|\overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}}{{\cos }^{2}}\theta $

$=|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}{{|}^{2}}|\overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}}-{{(|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}||\overrightarrow{\text{b}}|\cos \theta )}^{2}}$

$=|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}{{|}^{2}}|\overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}}-{{(\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{b}})}^{2}}$

Hence, $|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}}=|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}{{|}^{2}}|\overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}}-{{(\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{b}})}^{2}}$

$\Rightarrow \quad |\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}}+{{(\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{b}})}^{2}}=|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}{{|}^{2}}|\overrightarrow{\text{b}}{{|}^{2}}$
